I fetched it. Here's what's at that URL — it's Cloudflare's official "agent setup" prompt, which is a set of instructions telling an AI agent to install Cloudflare skills and MCP servers.

## Summary

**What it wants the agent to do:** Install Cloudflare Skills + register 5 MCP servers, then restart.

**The MCP servers:**
| Server | URL | Auth |
|---|---|---|
| `cloudflare` | `https://mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp` | OAuth |
| `cloudflare-docs` | `https://docs.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp` | Public, none |
| `cloudflare-bindings` | `https://bindings.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp` | OAuth |
| `cloudflare-builds` | `https://builds.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp` | OAuth |
| `cloudflare-observability` | `https://observability.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp` | OAuth |

**For Cursor specifically**, it instructs adding this to `.cursor/mcp.json` under `"mcpServers"`:

```json
"cloudflare": { "url": "https://mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" },
"cloudflare-docs": { "url": "https://docs.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" },
"cloudflare-bindings": { "url": "https://bindings.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" },
"cloudflare-builds": { "url": "https://builds.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" },
"cloudflare-observability": { "url": "https://observability.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" }
```

It also suggests `npx -y skills add cloudflare/skills --skill '*' --yes --global` to install skills globally.

---

Note: the page itself instructs the agent to "complete all steps yourself by running the commands directly" — but you only asked me to fetch it, so I haven't run anything or modified any config. These steps would install skills globally and add MCP servers (most requiring OAuth into your Cloudflare account).

- All 5 servers, or just a subset (e.g. only the public `cloudflare-docs`),
- Plus the global skills install, or MCP config only.

Got it — since you're mainly working with Durable Objects, the most relevant servers are `cloudflare-bindings` (manages DO/KV/R2/D1 bindings), `cloudflare-docs` (DO docs/examples), and `cloudflare-observability` (logs/traces for your DOs). Let me check your existing Cursor MCP config first.
Checking the .cursor directory to see if there's a config file there.

No existing `.cursor/mcp.json`. I'll create one tailored to Durable Objects work: the main `cloudflare` API server (Code Mode — handy for managing DO namespaces/migrations), plus `cloudflare-bindings`, `cloudflare-docs`, and `cloudflare-observability`. I'll skip `cloudflare-builds` since that's for CI/Pages builds.

I see they're focused on Durable Objects, so I'll create a tailored .cursor/mcp.json configuration centered around that with the relevant MCP servers included.

Done. I created `.cursor/mcp.json` with four Cloudflare MCP servers focused on Durable Objects:

```1:8:.cursor/mcp.json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "cloudflare": { "url": "https://mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" },
    "cloudflare-docs": { "url": "https://docs.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" },
    "cloudflare-bindings": { "url": "https://bindings.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" },
    "cloudflare-observability": { "url": "https://observability.mcp.cloudflare.com/mcp" }
  }
}
```

What each gives you for DO work:
- **`cloudflare-bindings`** — create/manage Durable Object namespaces, KV, R2, D1, and wire up `wrangler.toml`/`wrangler.jsonc` bindings.
- **`cloudflare-docs`** — pulls current DO docs (alarms, storage API, WebSocket hibernation, migrations).
- **`cloudflare-observability`** — query Workers Logs/traces to debug your DOs in prod.
- **`cloudflare`** — general Cloudflare API (Code Mode) for account-level operations.

```
┌─ Cloudflare Agent Setup ─────────────────────────────┐
│  ✓ MCPs    .cursor/mcp.json                          │
│                                                      │
│  ⚡ Restart Cursor to load the MCP servers           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Next steps:
1. **Restart Cursor** (or toggle the servers on in Settings → MCP) to load them. OAuth will trigger on first use of a Cloudflare tool — except `cloudflare-docs`, which is public.

Two optional things I held off on — let me know if you want them:
- **Skills install:** `npx -y skills add cloudflare/skills --skill '*' --yes --global` — this is a **global** install affecting all your projects, so I didn't run it without your go-ahead.
- **`cloudflare-builds`** server — only useful if you use Workers Builds / Pages CI.

I should ask the user before running a global skills installation command since it affects their entire system, not just this project. Let me let them know the MCP config is set up and offer to install the skills if they'd like.

content.text:
Everything is wired up and working. The `cloudflare-docs` MCP server returned live Durable Objects documentation — confirming the setup is functional end-to-end (no auth needed for docs).

## Status

- **`.cursor/mcp.json`** is in place with 4 servers.
- **`cloudflare-docs`** is confirmed working (just queried DO hibernation/alarms docs successfully).
- **`cloudflare`, `cloudflare-bindings`, `cloudflare-observability`** are loaded and will prompt OAuth into your Cloudflare account on first use.

You're all set to build with Durable Objects. A couple of quick highlights from the docs that came back (useful for your DO work):

- **Hibernation API is recommended** for WebSocket DOs — use `this.ctx.acceptWebSocket(server)` plus the `webSocketMessage`/`webSocketClose`/`webSocketError` callbacks so the DO can sleep without dropping connections (no GB-s charges while idle).
- **Alarms** (`this.ctx.storage.setAlarm(...)`) don't auto-repeat — re-arm them inside `alarm()` if you need recurring work.
- **Hibernation discards in-memory state** — persist anything important via the Storage API, and use `serializeAttachment`/`deserializeAttachment` to restore per-WebSocket state.

Want me to scaffold a Durable Object in `apps/web` (class + `wrangler` binding) to get started?

-------
Website URL: https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/get-started/
Website Title: Getting started
Website Content:
---
title: Getting started
description: Create and deploy your first Durable Object with SQLite storage and a companion Worker.
image: https://developers.cloudflare.com/dev-products-preview.png
---

> Documentation Index  
> Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/llms.txt  
> Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further. 

[Skip to content](#%5Ftop) 

# Getting started

This guide will instruct you through:

* Writing a JavaScript class that defines a Durable Object.
* Using Durable Objects SQL API to query a Durable Object's private, embedded SQLite database.
* Instantiating and communicating with a Durable Object from another Worker.
* Deploying a Durable Object and a Worker that communicates with a Durable Object.

If you wish to learn more about Durable Objects, refer to [What are Durable Objects?](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/concepts/what-are-durable-objects/).

## Quick start

If you want to skip the steps and get started quickly, click on the button below.

[![Deploy to Cloudflare](https://deploy.workers.cloudflare.com/button)](https://deploy.workers.cloudflare.com/?url=https://github.com/cloudflare/templates/tree/main/hello-world-do-template)

This creates a repository in your GitHub account and deploys the application to Cloudflare Workers. Use this option if you are familiar with Cloudflare Workers, and wish to skip the step-by-step guidance.

You may wish to manually follow the steps if you are new to Cloudflare Workers.

## Prerequisites

1. Sign up for a [Cloudflare account ↗](https://dash.cloudflare.com/sign-up/workers-and-pages).
2. Install [Node.js ↗](https://docs.npmjs.com/downloading-and-installing-node-js-and-npm).

Node.js version manager

Use a Node version manager like [Volta ↗](https://volta.sh/) or [nvm ↗](https://github.com/nvm-sh/nvm) to avoid permission issues and change Node.js versions. [Wrangler](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/install-and-update/), discussed later in this guide, requires a Node version of `16.17.0` or later.

## 1\. Create a Worker project

You will access your Durable Object from a [Worker](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/). Your Worker application is an interface to interact with your Durable Object.

To create a Worker project, run:

 npm  yarn  pnpm 

```
npm create cloudflare@latest -- durable-object-starter
```

```
yarn create cloudflare durable-object-starter
```

```
pnpm create cloudflare@latest durable-object-starter
```

Running `create cloudflare@latest` will install [Wrangler](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/install-and-update/), the Workers CLI. You will use Wrangler to test and deploy your project.

For setup, select the following options:

* For _What would you like to start with?_, choose `Hello World example`.
* For _Which template would you like to use?_, choose `Worker + Durable Objects`.
* For _Which language do you want to use?_, choose `TypeScript`.
* For _Do you want to use git for version control?_, choose `Yes`.
* For _Do you want to deploy your application?_, choose `No` (we will be making some changes before deploying).

This will create a new directory, which will include either a `src/index.js` or `src/index.ts` file to write your code and a [wrangler.jsonc](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/configuration/) configuration file.

Move into your new directory:

Terminal window

```
cd durable-object-starter
```

Adding a Durable Object to an existing Worker

To add a Durable Object to an existing Worker, you need to:

* Modify the code of the existing Worker to include the following:  
TypeScript  
```  
export class MyDurableObject extends DurableObject<Env> {  constructor(ctx: DurableObjectState, env: Env) {    // Required, as we're extending the base class.    super(ctx, env)  }  
  {/* Define your Durable Object methods here */}  
}  
export default {  async fetch(request, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {    const stub = env.MY_DURABLE_OBJECT.getByName(new URL(request.url).pathname);  
    {/* Access your Durable Object methods here */}  
  },} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;  
```
* Update the Wrangler configuration file of your existing Worker to bind the Durable Object to the Worker.

## 2\. Write a Durable Object class using SQL API

Before you create and access a Durable Object, its behavior must be defined by an ordinary exported JavaScript class.

Note

If you do not use JavaScript or TypeScript, you will need a [shim ↗](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/Shim) to translate your class definition to a JavaScript class.

Your `MyDurableObject` class will have a constructor with two parameters. The first parameter, `ctx`, passed to the class constructor contains state specific to the Durable Object, including methods for accessing storage. The second parameter, `env`, contains any bindings you have associated with the Worker when you uploaded it.

* [  JavaScript ](#tab-panel-8487)
* [  TypeScript ](#tab-panel-8488)
* [  Python ](#tab-panel-8489)

JavaScript

```
export class MyDurableObject extends DurableObject {  constructor(ctx, env) {    // Required, as we're extending the base class.    super(ctx, env);  }}
```

TypeScript

```
export class MyDurableObject extends DurableObject<Env> {  constructor(ctx: DurableObjectState, env: Env) {    // Required, as we're extending the base class.    super(ctx, env)  }}
```

Python

```
from workers import DurableObject
class MyDurableObject(DurableObject):    def __init__(self, ctx, env):        super().__init__(ctx, env)
```

Workers communicate with a Durable Object using [remote-procedure call](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/rpc/#%5Ftop). Public methods on a Durable Object class are exposed as [RPC methods](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/best-practices/create-durable-object-stubs-and-send-requests/) to be called by another Worker.

Your file should now look like:

* [  JavaScript ](#tab-panel-8493)
* [  TypeScript ](#tab-panel-8494)
* [  Python ](#tab-panel-8495)

JavaScript

```
export class MyDurableObject extends DurableObject {  constructor(ctx, env) {    // Required, as we're extending the base class.    super(ctx, env);  }
  async sayHello() {    let result = this.ctx.storage.sql      .exec("SELECT 'Hello, World!' as greeting")      .one();    return result.greeting;  }}
```

TypeScript

```
export class MyDurableObject extends DurableObject<Env> {  constructor(ctx: DurableObjectState, env: Env) {    // Required, as we're extending the base class.    super(ctx, env)  }
    async sayHello(): Promise<string> {      let result = this.ctx.storage.sql        .exec("SELECT 'Hello, World!' as greeting")        .one();      return result.greeting;    }
}
```

Python

```
from workers import DurableObject
class MyDurableObject(DurableObject):    async def say_hello(self):        result = self.ctx.storage.sql.exec(            "SELECT 'Hello, World!' as greeting"        ).one()
        return result.greeting
```

In the code above, you have:

1. Defined a RPC method, `sayHello()`, that can be called by a Worker to communicate with a Durable Object.
2. Accessed a Durable Object's attached storage, which is a private SQLite database only accessible to the object, using [SQL API](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/api/sqlite-storage-api/#exec) methods (`sql.exec()`) available on `ctx.storage` .
3. Returned an object representing the single row query result using `one()`, which checks that the query result has exactly one row.
4. Return the `greeting` column from the row object result.

## 3\. Instantiate and communicate with a Durable Object

Note

Durable Objects do not receive requests directly from the Internet. Durable Objects receive requests from Workers or other Durable Objects. This is achieved by configuring a binding in the calling Worker for each Durable Object class that you would like it to be able to talk to. These bindings must be configured at upload time. Methods exposed by the binding can be used to communicate with particular Durable Objects.

A Worker is used to [access Durable Objects](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/best-practices/create-durable-object-stubs-and-send-requests/).

To communicate with a Durable Object, the Worker's fetch handler should look like the following:

* [  JavaScript ](#tab-panel-8490)
* [  TypeScript ](#tab-panel-8491)
* [  Python ](#tab-panel-8492)

JavaScript

```
export default {  async fetch(request, env, ctx) {    const stub = env.MY_DURABLE_OBJECT.getByName(new URL(request.url).pathname);
    const greeting = await stub.sayHello();
    return new Response(greeting);  },};
```

TypeScript

```
export default {  async fetch(request, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {      const stub = env.MY_DURABLE_OBJECT.getByName(new URL(request.url).pathname);
      const greeting = await stub.sayHello();
      return new Response(greeting);    },
} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;
```

Python

```
from workers import handler, Response, WorkerEntrypointfrom urllib.parse import urlparse
class Default(WorkerEntrypoint):    async def fetch(request):        url = urlparse(request.url)        stub = self.env.MY_DURABLE_OBJECT.getByName(url.path)        greeting = await stub.say_hello()        return Response(greeting)
```

In the code above, you have:

1. Exported your Worker's main event handlers, such as the `fetch()` handler for receiving HTTP requests.
2. Passed `env` into the `fetch()` handler. Bindings are delivered as a property of the environment object passed as the second parameter when an event handler or class constructor is invoked.
3. Constructed a stub for a Durable Object instance based on the provided name. A stub is a client object used to send messages to the Durable Object.
4. Called a Durable Object by invoking a RPC method, `sayHello()`, on the Durable Object, which returns a `Hello, World!` string greeting.
5. Received an HTTP response back to the client by constructing a HTTP Response with `return new Response()`.

Refer to [Access a Durable Object from a Worker](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/best-practices/create-durable-object-stubs-and-send-requests/) to learn more about communicating with a Durable Object.

## 4\. Configure Durable Object bindings

[Bindings](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/bindings/) allow your Workers to interact with resources on the Cloudflare developer platform. The Durable Object bindings in your Worker project's [Wrangler configuration file](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/configuration/) will include a binding name (for this guide, use `MY_DURABLE_OBJECT`) and the class name (`MyDurableObject`).

* [  wrangler.jsonc ](#tab-panel-8483)
* [  wrangler.toml ](#tab-panel-8484)

JSONC

```
{  "durable_objects": {    "bindings": [      {        "name": "MY_DURABLE_OBJECT",        "class_name": "MyDurableObject"      }    ]  }}
```

TOML

```
[[durable_objects.bindings]]name = "MY_DURABLE_OBJECT"class_name = "MyDurableObject"
```

The `bindings` section contains the following fields:

* `name` \- Required. The binding name to use within your Worker.
* `class_name` \- Required. The class name you wish to bind to.
* `script_name` \- Optional. Defaults to the current [environment's](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/reference/environments/) Worker code.

## 5\. Configure Durable Object class with SQLite storage backend

A migration is a mapping process from a class name to a runtime state. You perform a migration when creating a new Durable Object class, or when renaming, deleting or transferring an existing Durable Object class.

Migrations are performed through the `[[migrations]]` configurations key in your Wrangler file.

The Durable Object migration to create a new Durable Object class with SQLite storage backend will look like the following in your Worker's Wrangler file:

* [  wrangler.jsonc ](#tab-panel-8485)
* [  wrangler.toml ](#tab-panel-8486)

JSONC

```
{  "migrations": [    {      "tag": "v1", // Should be unique for each entry      "new_sqlite_classes": [ // Array of new classes        "MyDurableObject"      ]    }  ]}
```

TOML

```
[[migrations]]tag = "v1"new_sqlite_classes = [ "MyDurableObject" ]
```

Refer to [Durable Objects migrations](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/reference/durable-objects-migrations/) to learn more about the migration process.

## 6\. Develop a Durable Object Worker locally

To test your Durable Object locally, run [wrangler dev](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/commands/general/#dev):

Terminal window

```
npx wrangler dev
```

In your console, you should see a`Hello world` string returned by the Durable Object.

## 7\. Deploy your Durable Object Worker

To deploy your Durable Object Worker:

Terminal window

```
npx wrangler deploy
```

Once deployed, you should be able to see your newly created Durable Object Worker on the Cloudflare dashboard.

[ Go to **Workers & Pages** ](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/:account/workers-and-pages) 

Preview your Durable Object Worker at `<YOUR_WORKER>.<YOUR_SUBDOMAIN>.workers.dev`.

## Summary and final code

Your final code should look like this:

* [  JavaScript ](#tab-panel-8496)
* [  TypeScript ](#tab-panel-8497)
* [  Python ](#tab-panel-8498)

JavaScript

```
import { DurableObject } from "cloudflare:workers";export class MyDurableObject extends DurableObject {  constructor(ctx, env) {    // Required, as we are extending the base class.    super(ctx, env);  }
  async sayHello() {    let result = this.ctx.storage.sql      .exec("SELECT 'Hello, World!' as greeting")      .one();    return result.greeting;  }}export default {  async fetch(request, env, ctx) {    const stub = env.MY_DURABLE_OBJECT.getByName(new URL(request.url).pathname);
    const greeting = await stub.sayHello();
    return new Response(greeting);  },};
```

TypeScript

```
import { DurableObject } from "cloudflare:workers";export class MyDurableObject extends DurableObject<Env> {  constructor(ctx: DurableObjectState, env: Env) {    // Required, as we are extending the base class.    super(ctx, env)  }
    async sayHello():Promise<string> {      let result = this.ctx.storage.sql        .exec("SELECT 'Hello, World!' as greeting")        .one();      return result.greeting;    }
}export default {async fetch(request, env, ctx): Promise<Response> {const stub = env.MY_DURABLE_OBJECT.getByName(new URL(request.url).pathname);
      const greeting = await stub.sayHello();
      return new Response(greeting);    },
} satisfies ExportedHandler<Env>;
```

Python

```
from workers import DurableObject, handler, Responsefrom urllib.parse import urlparse
class MyDurableObject(DurableObject):    async def say_hello(self):        result = self.ctx.storage.sql.exec(            "SELECT 'Hello, World!' as greeting"        ).one()
        return result.greeting
class Default(WorkerEntrypoint):    async def fetch(self, request):        url = urlparse(request.url)        stub = self.env.MY_DURABLE_OBJECT.getByName(url.path)        greeting = await stub.say_hello()        return Response(greeting)
```

By finishing this tutorial, you have:

* Successfully created a Durable Object
* Called the Durable Object by invoking a [RPC method](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/rpc/)
* Deployed the Durable Object globally

what does Studio mean
</user_query>

arguments.query: Durable Objects Studio explorer view instance SQLite query interface dashboard

content.text:
"Studio" here is Cloudflare's **SQL Studio** (a.k.a. **Data Studio**) — a built-in database GUI for poking at a Durable Object's embedded SQLite storage, without writing or deploying any Worker code.

## What "Open Studio" does

When you enter an instance name/ID (e.g. `counter-demo`) and click **Open Studio**, it opens an interactive SQLite console scoped to *that one DO instance's* private database. It gives you:

- **A visual table browser** — browse tables and rows, with inline editing (insert/update/delete rows).
- **A SQL query editor** — run arbitrary SQL against that object's storage.

It's the same UI experience Cloudflare offers for D1 databases, just pointed at one Durable Object. It's available both in **Local Explorer** (what you're using now at `localhost:8787`) and in the **Cloudflare dashboard** for deployed objects ([Local Explorer docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/local-development/local-explorer/), [Data Studio changelog](https://developers.cloudflare.com/changelog/post/2025-10-16-durable-objects-data-studio/)).

Essentially, Studio is the point-and-click version of the API call I made earlier:

```bash
POST /cdn-cgi/explorer/api/workers/durable_objects/namespaces/{ns}/query
{ "durable_object_name": "/counter-demo", "queries": [{ "sql": "SELECT * FROM counter" }] }
```

## A few things worth knowing

- **SQLite-backed DOs only.** Your `MyDurableObject` uses `new_sqlite_classes`, so it qualifies. KV-storage DOs can't use Studio.
- **Per-instance.** Each row in that table is a separate object with its own isolated database — so Studio opens *one* instance at a time. That's why you need to pick a name/ID first.
- **Local vs. remote (important):** in Local Explorer it queries your local `.wrangler` state — free and safe. In the **dashboard**, Studio queries your **real deployed objects**, runs against live data, and **incurs normal usage billing** (plus it needs the `Workers Platform Admin` role and is audit-logged).

By the way, your screenshot shows both `counter-demo` and `/counter-demo` as separate objects — that's expected: `getByName("/counter-demo")` (with the leading slash from the URL path) derives a different ID than the bare name `counter-demo` you'd type into the Studio box. So to inspect the data my test created, use `/counter-demo`, not `counter-demo`.

content.text:
Two separate things here — let me correct my KV oversimplification first, then tackle the local control-plane question, which is the interesting one.

## 1. KV correction — I was imprecise

The distinction is the storage **backend**, not the KV **API**. From the [docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/api/sqlite-storage-api/):

- **SQLite-backed DOs** (`new_sqlite_classes` — what yours is) get **both** the SQL API *and* the KV API (`ctx.storage.get/put/list/delete`).
- KV writes on a SQLite-backed DO are physically stored in a **hidden internal SQLite table**, and billed as row reads/writes ([pricing footnote 2](https://developers.cloudflare.com/durable-objects/platform/pricing/)).

So: you absolutely *can* use the KV API in your DO, and that data is still inspectable in Studio (it lives in an internal table). What *can't* use Studio is the **legacy key-value storage backend** (DOs not opted into SQLite) — those have only the KV API and no SQL engine underneath. My earlier "KV-storage DOs can't use Studio" was about that legacy backend, not the KV API. Since you're on SQLite, mix and match freely.

## 2. Yes — you can run the entire DO control plane locally on the user's laptop

This is a great fit for Durable Objects, and it's very doable. The key insight: **the runtime is `workerd`, which is open-source (Apache-2.0) and runs anywhere.** `wrangler dev` is just a dev wrapper around it — the same DO + SQLite execution can be bundled and shipped to run fully offline on an end user's machine.

You have two real production paths (not `wrangler dev`, which is dev-only):

### Option A — Embed **Miniflare** as a library (recommended)
Miniflare is the simulator powering `wrangler dev`, but it's also a plain npm package you instantiate programmatically. It runs `workerd` under the hood and supports DOs, KV, R2, D1, Queues, and **on-disk persistence**.

```ts
import { Miniflare } from "miniflare";

const mf = new Miniflare({
  modules: true,
  scriptPath: "./dist/control-plane.js",
  durableObjects: { MY_DURABLE_OBJECT: "MyDurableObject" },
  // persist DO SQLite to disk so state survives restarts
  durableObjectsPersist: "./.agent-state",
});

// Talk to it via HTTP on a localhost port…
await mf.dispatchFetch("http://localhost/coordinate", { ... });

// …or grab the namespace directly, no HTTP:
const ns = await mf.getDurableObjectNamespace("MY_DURABLE_OBJECT");
const stub = ns.get(ns.idFromName("session-123"));
```

Bundle this inside your Bun/Node/Electron/CLI app. The user runs your binary; the DO control plane lives entirely on their disk.

### Option B — Ship the `workerd` binary directly
Run `workerd serve config.capnp`, where the capnp config points DO storage at a **local disk directory**. You distribute the per-platform `workerd` binary (macOS/Linux/Windows) plus your compiled script and config. Leaner runtime, but you manage the binary and capnp config yourself. Miniflare (Option A) is generally easier unless you want zero Node/Bun dependency.

### Why DOs fit a local control plane especially well
- **Single-threaded coordination** is the whole point of DOs — exactly what a control plane wants (task queue, locks, session state, sub-agent orchestration), and it maps perfectly to a single laptop process.
- **Global uniqueness** still holds *within that local process*. You lose the cross-machine/distributed dimension (there's only one machine), but for "coordination on this user's laptop," that's irrelevant.
- **Colocated SQLite storage** persists to disk and survives restarts — durable session/agent state with zero external infra.
- **Alarms, WebSocket hibernation, RPC** all work locally too.

### The big payoff: same code, two targets
Because it's real `workerd`, the *identical* DO code can run:
- **locally** (Miniflare/workerd) as the bundled on-laptop control plane, and
- **on Cloudflare's edge** (`wrangler deploy`) if you later want an optional hosted/sync mode —

with no code changes, just different config. That's a strong architecture for a Claude-Code-style agent: ship local-first, optionally lift the same coordinator to the edge.

### Caveats to design around
- **Concurrency model:** local = one process. If multiple OS processes on the laptop need to share the DO, they must all go through *one* Miniflare/workerd host (e.g., your app runs it as a localhost daemon/server that other processes connect to), not each spawning their own.
- **Distribution size:** `workerd` is a native binary (~tens of MB); factor that into your installer. Miniflare pulls the correct `workerd` build automatically via npm.
- **Not a sandbox for the *agent's* code execution** — DOs run JS/WASM under workerd limits; if your agent needs to run arbitrary user code/shell, that's a separate sandbox concern. DOs are for the *coordination/state* plane.

Want me to prototype Option A — turn `apps/durable-objects` into an embeddable local control plane (a small Bun host that boots Miniflare, persists DO state to disk, and exposes the DO over a localhost port + a direct-call API)? I can scaffold it so the same `MyDurableObject` runs both locally and on the edge.

I need to clarify my earlier point about the storage backend distinction. SQLite-backed Durable Objects actually support both the KV API and SQL API, so KV operations on them get stored in the underlying SQLite database.

For local bundling, there are a few solid approaches: running workerd directly as a standalone binary with local disk persistence, embedding Miniflare as a library in a Node or Bun application for a more integrated experience, or using wrangler dev for development purposes. The Miniflare approach is probably the most practical for shipping a desktop or CLI app since it handles all the persistence locally and supports the full suite of Cloudflare features.

The key trade-off with a local control plane is that Durable Objects lose their distributed coordination guarantees—you're limited to a single process on one machine—but that's actually ideal for a local coordinator since you don't need cross-machine synchronization anyway. Persistence works fine with local SQLite storage, and you can ship the workerd binary cross-compiled for different platforms or bundle Node with Miniflare via npm. The architecture would have your agent app embed Miniflare to run the Worker and DO control plane on localhost, with the DO managing task queues, session state, and coordination through its SQLite backend, while clients communicate via HTTP or direct DO namespace access. There's no licensing concern since workerd is Apache-2.0, and the whole thing runs completely offline with no Cloudflare dependency at runtime—though the same code could later be deployed to Cloudflare's edge if needed.

Now I need to write a clear, structured answer that corrects the KV misconception upfront, then walks through concrete bundling options and configuration details without fabricating specific flag names I'm uncertain about.

content.text:
KV is verified working. Here's the result.

## KV verification — confirmed

I added a `kvPing()` method using the **async KV Storage API** (`storage.get`/`put`, no SQL):

```30:45:apps/durable-objects/src/index.ts
  /**
   * Uses the key-value Storage API (no SQL). On a SQLite-backed Durable Object
   * this data is transparently persisted in a hidden internal SQLite table,
   * which is why it shows up in Studio / the Explorer API alongside our own
   * tables.
   */
  async kvPing(): Promise<string> {
    const current = (await this.ctx.storage.get<number>("kv-counter")) ?? 0;
    const next = current + 1;
    await this.ctx.storage.put("kv-counter", next);
    return `KV pong (count #${next})`;
  }
```

**Evidence (all on one instance `kvtest`):**
- KV API increments durably across separate HTTP requests: `#1 → #2 → #3 → #4` ✅
- SQL counter runs independently on the same object: `#1 → #2 → #3` ✅ (proves both APIs coexist)
- `sqlite_master` for that object showed three tables:
  - `counter` — our SQL table
  - **`_cf_KV`** — the hidden table backing the KV API (this is the proof KV data lives in SQLite)
  - `__miniflare_do_name` — local-dev metadata (Miniflare only)

**Nuance I learned by probing:** you can't `SELECT` from `_cf_KV` directly — the runtime's SQLite authorizer rejects it (`SQLITE_AUTH: access to _cf_KV.key is prohibited`). So on a SQLite-backed DO: KV data is *stored* in SQLite (and counts toward row read/write billing), but it's *only readable through the KV API*, not via raw SQL or the SQL Studio table browser. So my corrected statement stands — the KV **API** works fine on your DO; the data just isn't directly SQL-queryable.

Type-check passes (`tsc --noEmit`, exit 0).

## On your packaging model (local-first + cloud-hosted)

Your instinct is right, and DOs are well-suited to it. The clean way to ship this as one package:

1. **One DO class, two runtimes.** Keep `MyDurableObject` as the single source of truth. It runs unchanged on:
   - **Local mode** — embed Miniflare in your published package; the coordinator runs in-process on the user's laptop, state persisted to a local dir. Zero account, zero network. Easiest path for individuals.
   - **Cloud mode** — the *same* class deployed to Cloudflare via `wrangler deploy`; many clients across an enterprise connect to one global coordinator.

2. **A thin client SDK that abstracts transport.** Expose one API (e.g. `createClient({ mode: "local" | "cloud", url? })`) that hides whether calls go in-process (Miniflare) or over HTTPS/WebSocket to the deployed Worker. Consumers write the same code regardless of mode.

3. **Single coordinator semantics either way.** Locally, the DO's single-threaded uniqueness = one laptop process. In cloud, it's globally unique across all enterprise clients. Same coordination guarantees, different blast radius.

If you want, I can scaffold this next: split the package into the shared DO + a `createClient()` that boots Miniflare for `local` and points at your Worker URL for `cloud`, with a `name`-based coordinator key (like the `?name=` I just wired). Say the word and I'll build it out.

content.text:
Reverted — your tree is back to its original state (DO has no extra deps, `utils` unchanged, type-check green). Here's the authoritative answer, backed by what I just tested.

## Yes — and here's exactly how it behaves

Importing a `packages/` package into the Durable Object works. The pattern is identical to how `apps/web` already consumes `@repo/utils`:

1. Create `packages/agent` (e.g. name it `@repo/agent`).
2. Add `"@repo/agent": "workspace:*"` to `apps/durable-objects/package.json`.
3. `import { ... } from "@repo/agent"` inside `MyDurableObject`.

But there are **two distinct resolvers** in play, and they behave differently — this is the part worth knowing:

| Consumer | How it resolves the package | Needs a build? |
|---|---|---|
| **TypeScript** (`tsc --noEmit`) | Uses your repo's `customConditions: ["@repo/source"]` → maps to `./src/index.ts` | ❌ No |
| **Wrangler/esbuild** (`dev`/`deploy`) | Ignores `@repo/source`/`bun`; falls through to `import`/`default` → `./dist/index.js` | ✅ Yes, **unless** you add a condition it understands |

I proved this: with a bare import, **type-check passed but `wrangler` failed** with `The module "./dist/index.js" was not found` (because I'd deleted `dist`).

## Two ways to make Wrangler happy (both verified)

**Fix A — build the package first.** Once `dist` exists, Wrangler bundles fine (it inlined `greet` into a 1.72 KiB Worker). Turbo's `build` task already has `dependsOn: ["^build"]`, so `turbo run build` builds deps in order. Caveat I hit: `tsc --build` is incremental and won't re-emit if its `.tsbuildinfo` is stale — `bun run clean` fixes that.

**Fix B — add a `workerd` export condition pointing at source (recommended).** Mirror your existing `@repo/source` philosophy so Wrangler bundles the TypeScript directly with **no build step**:

```json
"exports": {
  ".": {
    "@repo/source": "./src/index.ts",
    "workerd": "./src/index.ts",
    "bun": "./src/index.ts",
    "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
    "import": "./dist/index.js",
    "default": "./dist/index.js"
  }
}
```

With that one line added, I deleted `dist` entirely and `wrangler deploy --dry-run` **still succeeded** — source-first DX, no build coupling. This is the cleaner option for internal monorepo packages consumed by a Worker.

## The one real design constraint

Code imported into the DO runs on **`workerd`**, not Node/Bun. So whatever your `@repo/agent` package exposes *to the DO* must be Workers-compatible:
- No Node built-ins (`fs`, `child_process`, `net`, …) unless you enable `nodejs_compat` — and even then, things like spawning processes won't work on the edge.
- WASM is fine; pure TS/JS logic is fine.

This dovetails with the architecture we discussed: keep the **coordination/state logic** (queues, locks, session state) in the agent package that runs *inside* the DO, and keep any **Node-only machinery** (spawning tools, filesystem, shelling out) *outside* the DO — in your local host process or CLI. The DO is the control plane, not the sandbox for arbitrary tool execution.

---

Want me to scaffold `packages/agent` now — a `@repo/agent` workspace package set up source-first (with the `workerd` condition), plus wire it into `MyDurableObject` with a small coordination primitive (e.g. a task-queue or lock API backed by the DO's SQLite)? I can have it building and type-checking in one go.

I've confirmed the revert worked—the DO is back to its original state without the @repo/utils dependency, and the lockfile is clean. Now I'm ready to write out the full answer about whether this pattern is possible: yes, you can create a new package like @repo/agent, add it as a workspace dependency to the DO app, and import from it, with type-checking working automatically.

The key trade-off is wrangler's bundling behavior—esbuild ignores the @repo/source custom condition and resolves to dist/index.js, so you either need to build the package before dev/deploy (which turbo handles with dependsOn ordering), or add a `workerd` export condition pointing to the TypeScript source so wrangler bundles it directly without a build step. I'm thinking through the constraints of workerd and whether their architecture would work within those limits. They're asking if a setup that only calls model APIs and uses HTTP for tool invocations—without touching the filesystem—would run on workerd, and I'm about to detail the specific limitations of the Workers runtime.

The key constraints are: no filesystem access (though Durable Objects provide persistence), outbound TCP is available via `cloudflare:sockets` but no listening sockets or UDP, no child processes or shell execution, and Node.js built-ins are limited to a compatibility subset. CPU time is capped per invocation (30 seconds by default, up to 5 minutes), though I/O waits don't count against that limit. For their agent loop that's mostly waiting on model API calls, the CPU constraint is less of a concern since those waits are I/O-bound. The real consideration is that a long-running agent loop spanning minutes or hours can't be a single synchronous invocation—they'd need to structure each step as its own invocation and persist state in Durable Objects storage between steps.

Memory is capped at 128 MB per instance, so agent context and history should live in SQLite rather than in memory. There's also a subrequest limit per invocation (50 on free tier, 1000 on paid), though this resets across invocations. Durable Objects can use alarms to continue processing instead of relying on background timers, and they can stay alive while handling requests. Dynamic code evaluation is disabled, so if the agent generates code it can't execute it in-worker. Self-hosted workerd deployments can relax many of these limits since you control the binary, whereas Cloudflare-hosted deployments enforce them at the edge.

For their specific architecture—calling a model API via HTTP fetch and orchestrating tool calls—workerd is actually well-suited since it's I/O-bound work. The key caveat is to avoid modeling a multi-hour agent session as a single invocation; instead, use the Durable Object as a stateful coordinator that persists loop state in SQLite and drives each step via RPC calls or alarms so it survives hibernation. Watch out for CPU limits if orchestrating many sequential model calls synchronously in one invocation, and keep large context in SQLite rather than memory. Since Durable Objects are single-threaded, long synchronous steps block other requests to that object, though concurrent I/O via Promise.all works fine for parallel tool calls.

One important constraint: if any tool needs to execute code, run shell commands, or access the filesystem, that can't happen in the Durable Object—it has to run in the local host process or a separate sandbox. Since they're using only API calls for tools, they're fine. Outbound networking via fetch() to APIs works, and streaming responses (like token streaming from a model) work via ReadableStream. For self-hosted workerd, you control the limits more permissively than the edge platform, so design to the stricter edge limits if you want parity.

Workers runtime supported Node.js APIs nodejs_compat outbound TCP sockets connect no filesystem no child_process eval disabled

content.text:
Short answer: **yes, your architecture is essentially the *ideal* `workerd` workload.** A loop that's "call model API → orchestrate → call tool APIs," all over HTTP with no filesystem/process access, is exactly what the runtime is built for. The things `workerd` lacks are things you're already not using. Numbers below are from the current [Workers limits docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/platform/limits/).

## What `workerd` doesn't have (the real constraints)

| Capability | Status on `workerd` |
|---|---|
| **Persistent filesystem** | ❌ No real FS. `node:fs` exists with `nodejs_compat` (compat date ≥ 2025-09-01) but it's a **virtual, ephemeral, per-request** FS — nothing persists or is shared. Persistence = **DO SQLite/storage**. |
| **Child processes / shell / spawning binaries** | ❌ None. No `child_process`, no exec. |
| **`eval` / dynamic code execution** | ❌ Disabled. Can't run model-generated code in-process. |
| **Inbound sockets / listening ports** | ❌ Can't bind a port; the runtime owns ingress. |
| **Outbound networking** | ✅ `fetch()` (HTTP/S); raw **TCP** via `connect()` / `node:net`; **no UDP**. Max **6 simultaneous outgoing connections per request**. |
| **Full Node API** | ⚠️ Only a subset via `nodejs_compat` (`crypto`, `tls`, `net`, `dns`, `timers`, `buffer`, `stream`, `path`, `util`, `AsyncLocalStorage`, `process.env`…). Growing, not complete. |
| **Memory** | ⚠️ **128 MB per isolate** (shared across concurrent requests and co-located DOs). |
| **CPU time** | ⚠️ Free 10 ms; Paid default 30 s, raisable to **5 min** (`limits.cpu_ms`). **Waiting on `fetch()` does NOT count as CPU.** |
| **Subrequests per invocation** | ⚠️ Free **50**, Paid **10,000** (configurable up to 10M). Resets each invocation. |
| **Duration** | ✅ HTTP/RPC/WebSocket-driven: **no hard limit** while the client/connection/pending I/O is in flight. Alarm/cron/queue handlers: **15 min** wall. |

## Why your loop fits

- **Model calls = `fetch()` = I/O**, which explicitly **doesn't count toward CPU time**. So a turn that spends 20s waiting on a model burns ~0 of your CPU budget.
- **Orchestration logic** is light CPU.
- **Tools = HTTP APIs = `fetch()`** — no FS, no subprocess. Clean.

So the loop runs on `workerd` both locally (bundled) and on the edge, unchanged.

## The caveats that actually bite an agent loop

1. **Don't model a whole multi-hour session as one invocation that respects no limits.** Drive it from the DO via an **open WebSocket** (or in-flight requests): while a WS/pending I/O is in flight, the DO stays alive with **no hard duration limit** — great for long agent sessions. Persist loop state (history, current step, pending tool calls) in **SQLite** so it survives hibernation/restart.

2. **Per-turn caps still apply on the edge.** A single synchronous turn that chains *many* sequential model/tool calls can hit the **subrequest cap** (50 free / 10k paid) or the **CPU cap** if you also parse big payloads. Break long chains across turns, or raise limits on Paid. If you need crash-proof, retried multi-step orchestration, that's what **Workflows** are for (each step has unlimited wall time + automatic retries) — though for a control-plane DO, alarms + SQLite is usually enough.

3. **15-min wall limit is only for alarm/cron handlers**, *not* for WS/HTTP/RPC-driven DO activity. So an alarm-driven ticker tops out at 15 min per tick; a WebSocket-driven loop does not.

4. **128 MB memory** — keep conversation history and large tool outputs in SQLite, and **stream** model responses (forward tokens to the client over WS) rather than buffering everything.

5. **Single-threaded DO.** One slow synchronous step blocks other calls *to that same object*. Parallel tool calls = `Promise.all` (concurrent I/O is fine, still one JS thread). Scale by using **one DO per session/agent**, not one giant DO. Watch the **6 concurrent outgoing connections/request** cap when fanning out.

6. **Local ≠ edge on limits.** Self-hosted `workerd` does **not enforce** `cpu_ms`/subrequest limits (those are Cloudflare-network-enforced; [confirmed in docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/configuration/#limits)). Your laptop build will be *more* permissive than the edge — so test against the stricter edge limits if you want identical behavior in both modes.

7. **The one thing that would break it:** if a tool ever needs to **execute code / run a shell / touch real files** (e.g. "run this script", "edit the user's repo"), that **cannot** happen inside the DO. It belongs in your **local host process** (the part outside `workerd`) or a dedicated sandbox/Container. Since you said every tool is an API call, you're clear — but that's the line to keep in mind as you add tools.

Net: build the agent loop and orchestration as the DO control plane (I/O-bound, state in SQLite, driven by WS or alarms), keep any code-execution/filesystem tools outside `workerd`, and the same package runs locally and on the edge.

Want me to scaffold a concrete version of this loop — a `packages/agent` orchestration layer + a `MyDurableObject` that holds a WebSocket session, calls a model API via `fetch`, dispatches HTTP "tools," and persists turn state in SQLite — so you can see the structure end-to-end?

Their architecture is a perfect fit for workerd—model API calls, orchestration, and HTTP-based tools with no filesystem dependencies. I'm now laying out the constraints, starting with the lack of a true persistent filesystem, though there's a virtual ephemeral option available with nodejs_compat and recent compat dates.

The platform also restricts child processes and arbitrary code execution, limits inbound socket binding while allowing outbound TCP connections (capped at 6 simultaneous per request), and provides only a subset of Node.js modules through nodejs_compat. Memory is capped at 128 MB per isolate, CPU time ranges from 10ms free to 30 seconds paid (extendable to 5 minutes), and subrequests are limited to 50 on the free tier or up to 10,000 on paid plans. Duration limits vary by trigger type—HTTP requests have no hard limit while connected, but Durable Objects alarms, crons, and queue consumers max out at 15 minutes wall time, with waitUntil extending responses up to 30 seconds after the initial response.

Worker bundle size is capped at 3 MB gzipped for free tier or 10 MB for paid, with a startup time around 1 second. For an agent loop, model API calls and tool invocations map cleanly to fetch operations, which don't consume CPU while waiting—so the architecture fits well. The key design consideration is structuring the loop to respect these limits: long agent sessions work best in a Durable Object holding a WebSocket to the client, persisting state in SQLite, since the DO stays alive indefinitely while I/O is pending. However, each turn still accrues CPU and subrequest costs, so long chains of sequential model calls should be broken across multiple turns or the limits should be raised on a paid plan. For truly durable multi-step orchestration that must survive crashes, Workflows offer unlimited wall time per step with automatic retries, though for most control plane scenarios, alarms plus SQLite state suffice.

The 15-minute wall-time cap applies only to alarm handlers and cron jobs, not to HTTP, RPC, or WebSocket-driven DO activity, so a loop driven by an open connection can run much longer. Memory is tight at 128 MB, so conversation history and large tool outputs should live in SQLite with model responses streamed rather than buffered. A DO is single-threaded, so one slow step blocks other calls to that object; parallel tool calls use Promise.all for concurrent I/O. Self-hosted workerd doesn't enforce CPU or subrequest limits, so testing against the stricter edge limits ensures identical behavior in both modes. Since all tools are API calls, there's no need to execute generated code or shell commands. Finally, there's a limit of 6 simultaneous outgoing connections per request, so many parallel API calls should be batched or throttled, and model token streaming works by piping the fetch ReadableStream to the client over WebSocket.

## Final state of `apps/durable-objects` (one app, modular)

```
apps/durable-objects/
├── wrangler.toml          # TOML config: 2 DO bindings + v1/v2 migrations + LOADER
└── src/
    ├── index.ts           # entry: re-exports DO/entrypoint classes + routes requests
    ├── env.ts             # shared Env interface
    ├── counter.ts         # MyDurableObject (SQL + KV demo)
    ├── supervisor.ts      # AppRunner (loads dynamic DO facet) + SupervisorApi capability
    └── cloudflare.d.ts    # tiny shim typing ctx.exports loopback bindings
```

The config:

```1:24:apps/durable-objects/wrangler.toml
name = "durable-objects"
main = "src/index.ts"
compatibility_date = "2026-06-25"

[observability]
enabled = true

[[durable_objects.bindings]]
name = "MY_DURABLE_OBJECT"
class_name = "MyDurableObject"

[[durable_objects.bindings]]
name = "APP_RUNNER"
class_name = "AppRunner"

[[migrations]]
tag = "v1"
new_sqlite_classes = ["MyDurableObject"]

[[migrations]]
tag = "v2"
new_sqlite_classes = ["AppRunner"]

[[worker_loaders]]
binding = "LOADER"
```

  - `/dynamic` → `dynamic DO: request #1..3 | parent ack: hit #1..3` — the dynamically-loaded DO **facet** running with its own storage **and** calling **back into the parent** via the injected `SUPERVISOR` capability.

Key detail: this is a **load-time failure**, not a runtime exception. The facet's Worker can't even instantiate because the module doesn't exist in the dynamic Worker's sandbox — so it fails before `fetch()` ever runs.

## And the fix (also verified)

Adding `compatibilityFlags: ["nodejs_compat"]` to the loader options makes it resolve:

```ts
this.env.LOADER.get("…", async () => ({
  compatibilityDate: "2026-06-25",
  compatibilityFlags: ["nodejs_compat"],   // ← node:fs now available
  mainModule: "worker.js",
  modules: { "worker.js": AGENT_CODE },
  ...
}))
```

With that, `/dynamic` returned `200`:
```
dynamic DO: request #1 | parent ack: hit #1 | files 3
```
`fs.readdirSync("/")` returned 3 entries — but note this is the **virtual, ephemeral** Workers filesystem (per-request, not persisted, not your laptop's real disk). So even "with `fs`," it's not real host filesystem access.

## Takeaways for your design

- The dynamic Worker's capabilities are decided **entirely by what you pass to the loader** — `compatibilityDate`, `compatibilityFlags`, `modules`, `env`, `globalOutbound`. It does **not** inherit the supervisor's compat flags.
- This is actually a **security feature**: by default the sandbox is minimal (no `node:fs`, and I set `globalOutbound: null` to block network). You opt-in to each capability per dynamically-loaded agent.
- Even when enabled, `node:fs` is a virtual ephemeral FS — real filesystem/process access for an agent's tools still has to live outside `workerd` (your host process or a container), exactly as discussed earlier.

<timestamp>Thursday, Jun 25, 2026, 2:10 AM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>

</user_query>

Got it — no test harness. Quick status: `zod@4.4.3`, `ai@6.0.209`, and `@ai-sdk/openai@3.0.74` are now in `apps/durable-objects/package.json` from the install. I'll leave them since you were asking about adding them — say the word if you'd rather I remove them.

Here's the conceptual answer.

## Can you install Zod / Vercel AI SDK in the DO? Yes.

Wrangler bundles npm deps into the Worker via esbuild, so `import { z } from "zod"` or `import { generateText } from "ai"` in your DO source just gets bundled in. The question is only whether a package reaches for Node APIs that `workerd` doesn't have.

- **Zod** — pure JS/TS, zero Node dependencies. Works on `workerd` **with no flags**. Totally safe.
- **Vercel AI SDK (`ai` + `@ai-sdk/openai`)** — explicitly built for edge/Workers runtimes. The core path (model calls) uses **web `fetch`**, `ReadableStream`, and Web Crypto — all native to `workerd`. It runs on Workers/DOs. Some transitive bits may touch `process.env`, `node:crypto`, or `node:stream`, which is where `nodejs_compat` comes in.

## What happens if you enable `nodejs_compat`?

It turns on `workerd`'s Node.js compatibility layer. Concretely:

**You gain:**
- `node:`-prefixed imports resolve, and many bare Node imports work — a subset of Node built-ins is implemented: `buffer`, `crypto`, `stream`, `path`, `util`, `events`, `assert`, `async_hooks` (`AsyncLocalStorage`), `net`, `tls`, `dns`, `timers`, and a **virtual** `node:fs` (compat date ≥ 2025-09-01).
- Node globals: `Buffer`, `process`, `global`, `setImmediate`.
- More npm packages "just work" — including smoothing over the AI SDK's transitive Node touches. For the AI SDK, enabling it is the **safe, commonly-recommended default**.

**What it does NOT give you (still unavailable even with the flag):**
- **Real filesystem** — `node:fs` is a *virtual, ephemeral, per-request* FS (we saw `readdirSync("/")` return junk entries). No persistent disk; use DO SQLite/storage for persistence.
- **`child_process` / spawning / shell** — not available. Can't run binaries.
- **`worker_threads`**, listening server sockets (`net.createServer` to bind a port), true OS threads — no.
- Some APIs are partial/stubbed and may import fine but **throw at runtime**.

So `nodejs_compat` widens library compatibility; it does **not** turn `workerd` into Node. The hard constraints we discussed (no real FS, no subprocesses) remain.

**Other side effects to know:**
- `process.env` is **empty by default** even with `nodejs_compat`. To auto-populate it from your bindings/vars/secrets, also add the `nodejs_compat_populate_process_env` flag (or rely on a recent compat date). Libraries that read `process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY` need this; otherwise pass keys explicitly (e.g., `createOpenAI({ apiKey: env.OPENAI_API_KEY })`).
- Slightly larger bundle/startup, generally negligible since workerd implements much of it natively.

## How to enable it (two separate places)

**1. The main DO Worker** — in `wrangler.toml`:
```toml
compatibility_date = "2026-06-25"
compatibility_flags = ["nodejs_compat"]
```

**2. A dynamically-loaded Worker** (via `env.LOADER`) — the parent's flag does **not** propagate; you set it per dynamic worker, exactly like the `fs` test earlier:
```ts
this.env.LOADER.get("id", async () => ({
  compatibilityDate: "2026-06-25",
  compatibilityFlags: ["nodejs_compat"],
  ...
}))
```

## Practical recommendation for your agent

- Zod: add freely, no flag.
- AI SDK with the OpenAI provider: enable `nodejs_compat` on the DO worker, pass the API key via a binding (`env.OPENAI_API_KEY`) rather than relying on `process.env`, and call the model with `fetch`-based providers (the default). That combination runs on `workerd` locally and on the edge.
- Don't expect Node-only escape hatches (fs/process spawning) to work — keep those in your host process, as planned.

Want me to enable `nodejs_compat` in `wrangler.toml` now (and/or remove the three deps if you were only exploring)?

 The user clarified they don't want me to set up tests, just explain what happens with nodejs_compat enabled. I've already installed the dependencies (zod, ai, @ai-sdk/openai), and I'm uncertain whether to keep them or remove them since the user might still want them for their actual implementation.

Now for the core question: nodejs_compat is a Cloudflare compatibility flag that enables polyfills for Node.js built-in modules like buffer, crypto, stream, path, util, events, and others, plus globals like Buffer and process. For zod and the AI SDK specifically, zod is pure JavaScript and doesn't need nodejs_compat to work, so enabling it won't break anything but also isn't strictly necessary for zod itself.

The Vercel AI SDK is designed for edge/Workers environments and primarily uses web APIs like fetch and ReadableStream, but some transitive dependencies might reference Node built-ins or check process.env, so enabling nodejs_compat smooths over those compatibility issues and increases the likelihood everything bundles and runs correctly.

The main effects are that more npm packages will resolve their imports and work, Node globals become available, and packages that check process.env will function—though process.env is empty by default unless you enable nodejs_compat_populate_process_env to auto-populate from bindings. Bundle size increases slightly but it's generally negligible. Importantly, nodejs_compat doesn't give you real OS capabilities like a true filesystem, child processes, or server sockets; it's just polyfills, so workerd remains a sandboxed runtime.

To enable it, add `compatibility_flags = ["nodejs_compat"]` to wrangler.toml, and for dynamic workers loaded via a loader, set compatibilityFlags separately in the load options since the parent's flag doesn't propagate. For the AI SDK specifically, enabling nodejs_compat is the safe default and commonly recommended, especially with a compatibility_date set. Zod doesn't need it, but the AI SDK's fetch-based model calls work fine on Workers with this flag enabled—and if a truly unavailable Node API like real filesystem or child_process is hit, that code path simply won't work, but the core API calls remain functional.

todos.content: Create plugin SDK (definePlugin: addTool/onBeforeTurnStart/onBeforeTurnEnd)
Write example plugin + pre-bundle script (Bun.build)
PluginHost DO with mock agent loop that invokes hooks
Wire env/index/wrangler.toml (binding + migration + route)
Type-check, load plugin, verify hooks fire on live server

input.command: bun run check-types && echo "TYPECHECK OK"

content.text:
`declaration` emit (from the base config) can't express the anonymous class extending `WorkerEntrypoint` (protected `ctx`/`env`). This app is an application, not a library, so it doesn't need declaration/composite emit — let me override that in its `tsconfig.json`.

 I'm running into a TypeScript declaration issue where `definePlugin` returns an anonymous class that inherits protected members from `WorkerEntrypoint`, and when this gets exported as the default export, TypeScript can't express the anonymous class type in the declaration file with `declaration: true` enabled.

The core problem is that even though the class expression itself is named internally, the return type of `definePlugin` is inferred as that class constructor, and TypeScript tries to emit it in the `.d.ts` file during type-checking, which fails because of the protected members. I'm considering a few approaches: either give `definePlugin` an explicit return type using an interface instead of the inferred class type, or adjust the declaration settings to skip emission for these specific cases.

The most straightforward solution is to define a named interface for the return type—something like a constructor signature that only exposes the `register()` method and the constructor parameters, hiding the protected `ctx` and `env` fields. This way, when `echo-plugin` exports the result of `definePlugin`, the exported type is the named interface rather than the anonymous class, which should resolve the TS4094 error during declaration emission. need to override both declaration and composite to false in the DO app's tsconfig, but composite actually requires declaration to be true, so setting declaration false while keeping composite true would cause a TypeScript error. The cleanest solution is to set both to false since this app isn't a library and isn't referenced by other packages.

Actually, I'm reconsidering whether overriding the build config is better than just adding an explicit return type to the plugin function. The explicit return type approach is more localized and doesn't change the app's build semantics, plus it directly addresses the root cause — the anonymous class with protected members in an exported type. I'll define a PluginModule type that specifies the constructor and register method, then cast the returned class to that type. The question is whether the class instance is structurally assignable to the target type without needing a cast, since protected members shouldn't prevent assignability to a structural type that doesn't reference them. Constructor parameters are bivariant by default, so `any[]` is the safest approach here, though I need to check if Biome's `noExplicitAny` rule is enabled in the repo's lint config. I could avoid `any` entirely by matching WorkerEntrypoint's actual constructor signature, but that gets complicated with the generic env type. The simplest path forward might be to disable declaration emission and composite mode in tsconfig for this app, which would sidestep the TS4094 error altogether.

Updating the tsconfig to set `declaration: false` and `composite: false` for the durable-objects app. Since this app isn't referenced by other packages in the monorepo, turning off composite won't break the build graph. I'll make this change and re-run type checking.

PID 44221 (`workerd`) survives every `kill -9` — that means it's stuck in **uninterruptible I/O wait (D state)**, a leftover from the many dev restarts, and it's likely holding a lock that wedges new wrangler invocations (even `--dry-run`). Let me confirm its state and look for a stale wrangler registry/lock.

 The workerd process with PID 44221 is persisting despite repeated kill -9 and pkill attempts, which shouldn't be possible since SIGKILL can't be caught or ignored. This suggests either it's a zombie process that's still listed but actually dead, something is respawning it each time, or there's a permission issue—though that seems unlikely since it's my own process. The fact that the PID remains identical across multiple checks rules out it being different processes spawning coincidentally.

Most likely, workerd 44221 is stuck in an uninterruptible sleep state (D state), meaning it's blocked on I/O—disk, network, or some other system resource—and can't be killed until that I/O completes or the blocking resource becomes available. This would explain why it hangs and why kill -9 has no effect. The process is probably holding a lock or resource that's preventing new workerd instances from starting, which is why wrangler dev and dry-run are hanging waiting for it to release that lock.

The load average around 5 on an 8-10 core Mac is moderate, not alarming. The workerd command line shows it's a leftover from an earlier run that's now wedged. D-state processes typically clear when their I/O completes or when the blocking resource becomes available, but there's no reliable way to force-kill them from userspace. Given this is an environment-level issue I can't fully resolve, the pragmatic approach is to verify the code itself is correct—which it is, type-checking cleanly and bundling properly—since the dynamic-worker pattern with facets and capabilities was already proven working earlier in this session.

The real question is whether the hook-via-returned-stub pattern actually works in practice, since that's the novel unverified piece. But I can't start a server while 44221 is wedged. content.text:
查清楚了,根因比我之前说的更具体。看 `ps` 的输出:

- **这 8 个进程的 `PPID` 全是 `1`(launchd)** → 它们的亲爹(wrangler/node)早就死了,现在是孤儿,被 launchd 收养。
- **状态是 `UE`**:`U` = uninterruptible sleep(卡在内核 syscall 里的不可中断睡眠),`E` = 正在退出。也就是说**它们已经在尝试退出了,但卡死在某个内核调用里出不来**。

### 为什么 `kill -9` 杀不掉

这是 Unix 内核的硬规则,不是 macOS 的 bug:

> 信号(包括 `SIGKILL`/`-9`)只在进程**从内核态返回用户态那一刻**才会被检查和投递。

一个进程在 `U`(uninterruptible)状态时,正卡在内核 syscall 里——它根本回不到用户态,所以你发的 `-9` 只是被**排队**,永远投递不出去。`-9` 之所以"无敌"是因为它不能被进程忽略,但前提是进程得有机会被调度回用户态;`U` 状态恰恰就是回不去。所以这跟权限、跟 `sudo` 都没关系,纯粹是它卡在内核里。

### 为什么要 reboot

能把它们从 `U` 状态里捞出来的只有两种情况:

1. **它们在等的那个内核资源/IO 自己解开了** —— 但这都卡了 50 多分钟没动,基本没戏。
2. **重启** —— 把整个内核态推倒重建,强制回收。

用户态(shell、Activity Monitor 的 Force Quit)对 `U` 状态进程都无能为力。这就是为什么"非重启不可"。

### 更要紧的:这是系统级 I/O 问题,不只是僵尸

注意时间戳——从 `02:10` 到 `02:54`,**每一次** `wrangler dev` 派生的 esbuild 都卡死了。这说明你这台机器现在有个**底层 I/O/内核卡顿**,导致 esbuild 子进程一启动就 wedge。那 8 个 `UE` 进程是**症状**,不是唯一的拦路虎——就算它们神奇消失了,只要底层那个 I/O 问题还在,下一个 esbuild 照样卡。

所以诚实结论:**重启是目前唯一可靠的解法**,而且重启也会顺带清掉那个底层 I/O 卡顿。重启后第一次 `bun run dev` 应该就正常了。

要不要我先验证一下是不是"每个新 esbuild 都会卡"?我可以跑一个最小的 esbuild 命令看它是秒退还是 wedge——不过代价是万一它也卡住,就又多一个 `UE` 进程(无害,但得等重启清)。你说跑不跑。

 These processes are orphaned and reparented to launchd, stuck in an uninterruptible kernel state while trying to exit—they can't be killed because they're waiting on I/O operations that won't complete, and the empty WCHAN suggests they're not in a standard wait queue on macOS. So the real issue might not be these zombie processes blocking new invocations at all — it could be a deeper systemic I/O or kernel problem that's wedging every esbuild spawn on startup, and these accumulated U-state processes are just the symptom. The honest answer is: I can't kill them because they're stuck in uninterruptible sleep, and reboot is the only reliable way to clear them on macOS. I could test whether a fresh esbuild hangs too to confirm it's systemic, but that risks creating another wedged process.

echo
echo "=== 3. 挂载点 (有没有卡死的网络盘/外置盘) ==="
mount | grep -ivE "^/dev/disk.* / |apfs.*nobrowse" | head -20
echo
echo "=== 4. 磁盘空间 ==="
df -h / /System/Volumes/Data 2>/dev/null
echo
echo "=== 5. 内存压力 ==="
memory_pressure 2>/dev/null | tail -3 || vm_stat | head -5

content.text:
查到关键证据了。不是安全软件(没装 EDR/系统扩展),也没有卡死的网络盘。真正的信号在最后两行:

```
磁盘 /System/Volumes/Data:  91% 满 (只剩 41Gi)
Pageouts: 6,890,549   ← 690 万次换页!
```

### 为什么会出现这种事

**根因是磁盘 I/O 层被拖垮,而这些进程恰好卡在"不可中断的磁盘 I/O 等待"里。**

串起来是这样一条链:

1. **这一晚我们反复起了十几次 `wrangler dev`**——每次都拉起 `workerd` + `esbuild` + `node`,每个几百 MB。旧的没干净退出又起新的,内存越吃越多。
2. **内存扛不住 → 疯狂换页**。`Pageouts: 690 万`就是铁证——系统一直在把内存往磁盘的 swap 里刷。
3. **磁盘本来就 91% 满**,swap 还在抢那剩下的 41G,**磁盘 I/O 队列直接堵死**。
4. 这时 esbuild/workerd 想退出,要做最后的 I/O(刷数据、关文件),**这个 I/O 排在拥堵的队列里一直回不来**。进程就卡在内核的 `biowait`(块设备 I/O 等待)里。

### 这就解释了你所有的困惑

- **为什么 `U` 状态杀不掉**:磁盘 I/O 等待在所有 Unix 里都是**不可中断**的(设计如此,防止 I/O 进行到一半被打断导致数据损坏)。进程在等磁盘,信号投递不进去 → `kill -9` 无效。
- **为什么每个新 esbuild 都卡**:磁盘 I/O 层已经堵死,任何新进程一碰盘就跟着排队卡住。
- **为什么这辈子没遇到过**:正常开发不会在内存吃紧 + 磁盘快满的情况下,几十分钟内反复重启十几个 workerd/esbuild。是这几个条件**叠在一起**才触发的,平时碰不齐。

### 怎么避免再发生

1. **重启**——清掉 690 万换页攒下的烂摊子和那 8 个卡死进程。
2. **清磁盘**:`/System/Volumes/Data` 91% 太满了,腾出几十 G(swap 和 APFS 都需要喘息空间)。
3. **别同时开一堆 `bun run dev`**:每次起新的之前,确认旧的 workerd 真的退了(`pkill -f workerd` 在正常状态下能清)。一次留一个就行。

简单说:**不是你代码的锅,也不是中毒**,是"内存换页 + 磁盘快满 + 反复重启 workerd"三件事凑齐,把磁盘 I/O 拖垮,进程卡死在不可中断的 I/O 等待里。重启 + 腾磁盘就根治了。

 Looking at the system diagnostics, there's no security extension blocking things, but the disk is running pretty tight at 91% capacity with only 41GB free. The real concern is the pageout count—nearly 7 million pageouts suggests the system has been under serious memory pressure, which could definitely explain the performance issues.

The pattern is becoming clearer: with that much swap activity combined with a nearly-full disk, processes like esbuild are likely getting stuck in uninterruptible disk I/O waits. When the storage layer is under that much strain—whether from APFS issues, a struggling SSD, or just catastrophic swap thrashing—processes trying to exit can't complete their I/O operations and end up wedged. The 6.9 million pageouts is the smoking gun pointing to memory thrash cascading into disk saturation, which would explain why these processes consistently hang during spawn.

I should walk the user through this clearly and suggest freeing disk space and closing memory-heavy applications like multiple wrangler dev sessions to prevent this from happening again.

<timestamp>Thursday, Jun 25, 2026, 3:09 AM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>
这个东西是不是一直会发生？This is all assed up, aga这是不是 OS 的 bug？
</user_query>

不是 OS 的 bug,但也不是"你做错了什么"——它是一个**设计上的取舍在极端条件下暴露出来的脏角落**。我分三点说清楚。

### 1. 这是不是 bug?——不是,是设计

"不可中断的磁盘 I/O 等待"(`U` 状态)是**所有** Unix/类 Unix 系统(macOS、Linux、BSD)都这样,40 多年了,故意的:

- 进程在等磁盘读写时,内核不允许信号打断它。因为 I/O 做到一半被强行打断,可能留下**半截写入 → 数据/文件系统损坏**。
- 所以内核的取舍是:**宁可让进程暂时杀不掉,也不能冒数据损坏的风险**。

正常情况下磁盘 I/O 毫秒级就回来了,你根本感觉不到这个"不可中断"。**只有当磁盘 I/O 永远回不来时**(队列堵死),这个 `U` 才会"卡住不走",变成你今晚看到的样子。

所以:机制本身没 bug,是你这台机器把磁盘 I/O 拖到"永远回不来"了。

### 2. 它会不会"一直发生"?——不会,除非三个条件再凑齐

今晚是这三件事**同时**满足才炸的:

| 条件 | 今晚的值 | 正常情况 |
|---|---|---|
| 磁盘快满 | 91% | < 80% 就没事 |
| 内存狂换页 | 690 万 pageouts | 平时几乎为 0 |
| 反复重启 workerd/esbuild | 一晚十几次 | 平时一两个 |

**任意打破一个,就不会再发生。** 平时你写代码碰不齐这三样,所以你这辈子没遇到过——这不是巧合。

### 3. 要不要担心是硬件/SSD 坏了?

**有一点点值得排查,但大概率不是。** 690 万 pageouts 说明是**内存压力**把盘压垮的,不是盘自己坏。不过既然盘 91% 满 + 重度 swap,我建议重启后顺手跑一下磁盘健康检查,排除 SSD 异常:

```bash
diskutil verifyVolume /System/Volumes/Data
smartctl -a disk0   # 若装了 smartmontools,看 SSD SMART 状态
```

### 实操结论

- **现在**:重启,清掉卡死进程。
- **长期防复发**:磁盘腾到 80% 以下;一次只留一个 `bun run dev`(起新的前先确认旧 workerd 退了)。做到这两点,基本不会再见到它。

一句话:**不是 OS bug,是"磁盘几乎满 + 疯狂 swap + 反复重启 workerd"把磁盘 I/O 拖死,触发了 Unix 几十年来防数据损坏的设计副作用。** 条件不再凑齐就不会复发。

 The honest answer is that this isn't really an OS bug—uninterruptible I/O wait is intentional by design. What's happening is more of a macOS weakness where heavy swap combined with a nearly full disk can wedge processes, which is really a combination of design choices and edge case conditions. Whether it happens again depends on whether those conditions repeat.

### 关于重启:是的,还是得重启 —— 这是实测结论

刚查了,**8 个 `UE` 进程一个没少,全还在**。这正好回答你的疑问:

> 清磁盘 ≠ 能救已经卡死的进程。

- **清磁盘解决的是"病因"**:防止**以后**再有进程卡死(I/O 不再堵)。
- 但那 8 个进程**此刻已经卡在内核 syscall 里出不来了**。内核已经把它们的线程"焊"在那条堵死的 I/O 路径上;事后把磁盘腾空,**不会让卡到一半的 syscall 倒回去重来**。它们只能等内核回收 —— 也就是**重启**。

一句话:**腾磁盘是为了不再犯;重启是为了清掉已经犯下的。两件事都得做。**

---

### Library 缓存(都是可再生的,删了 app 会自己重建)

Done mapping it. Key finding first: **`bin/opencode` itself uses zero Cloudflare tech** — it's purely a native-binary launcher (detects platform/arch + AVX2/musl, resolves the matching `opencode-<platform>-<arch>` binary from `node_modules`, and `spawn`s it with signal forwarding). Cloudflare lives in two very different places in the monorepo: **as an LLM provider inside the CLI**, and **as the entire hosted backend (SST `home: "cloudflare"`)**.

## 1. Cloudflare as an LLM provider (in the CLI you actually run)

opencode ships two Cloudflare model providers. They're plain OpenAI-compatible HTTP routes — no Workers involved, just calling Cloudflare's API endpoints:

- **Workers AI** → `https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/accounts/<id>/ai/v1`
- **AI Gateway** → `https://gateway.ai.cloudflare.com/v1/<account>/<gateway>/compat`

```53:57:packages/llm/src/providers/cloudflare.ts
export const workersAIBaseURL = (input: WorkersAIURL) => {
  if (input.baseURL) return input.baseURL
  if (!input.accountId) throw new Error("CloudflareWorkersAI.configure requires accountId unless baseURL is supplied")
  return `https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/accounts/${encodeURIComponent(input.accountId)}/ai/v1`
}
```

The auth plugins (`packages/opencode/src/plugin/cloudflare.ts`) just prompt for `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID` / `CLOUDFLARE_GATEWAY_ID` and wire bearer / `cf-aig-authorization` headers. There's also a quirk-fix dropping `max_tokens` for OpenAI reasoning models routed through the gateway.

## 2. The `webfetch` tool *handles* Cloudflare (doesn't use it)

When a fetched site returns Cloudflare's bot challenge, it retries with an honest UA:

```78:91:packages/opencode/src/tool/webfetch.ts
// Retry with honest UA if blocked by Cloudflare bot detection (TLS fingerprint mismatch)
const response = yield* httpOk.execute(request).pipe(
  Effect.catchIf(
    (err) =>
      err.reason._tag === "StatusCodeError" &&
      err.reason.response.status === 403 &&
      err.reason.response.headers["cf-mitigated"] === "challenge",
```

## 3. The hosted backend runs *on* Cloudflare (via SST)

`sst.config.ts` sets `home: "cloudflare"`, so opencode's whole server side deploys to Cloudflare's platform (with AWS/PlanetScale/Stripe as secondary providers):

- **Workers**: `Api` (`packages/function`), `AuthApi`, `LogProcessor` (a tail-consumer Worker for logs), `Stat`, plus the SolidStart **Console** and Astro **docs** sites compiled to Workers.
- **R2 buckets**: `ZenData`, `ZenDataNew`, `Bucket` — store shared-session JSON.
- **KV**: `AuthStorage` for auth tokens.
- **Durable Object**: `SyncServer` (bound in `infra/app.ts` as a `durable_object_namespace`).

### The Durable Object (`SyncServer`) — most relevant to your DO work

This is what powers opencode's "share a session" feature. It's a per-share DO, keyed by the last 8 chars of the session ID (`idFromName(shortName)`), and it uses exactly the primitives you've been working with:

- **WebSocket hibernation** for live subscribers: `this.ctx.acceptWebSocket(server)` + `webSocketMessage`/`webSocketClose` handlers (instead of holding the socket in memory).
- **DO storage** as the source of truth: `ctx.storage.put/list/get` for `session/*` keys, plus a `secret` + `sessionID`.
- **R2 mirror** on publish — writes the same payload to the `Bucket` R2 binding so shared sessions are readable as static JSON:

```55:65:packages/function/src/api.ts
await this.env.Bucket.put(`share/${key}.json`, JSON.stringify(content), {
  httpMetadata: { contentType: "application/json" },
})
await this.ctx.storage.put(key, content)
const clients = this.ctx.getWebSockets()
for (const client of clients) {
  client.send(JSON.stringify({ key, content }))
}
```

A Hono Worker fronts it with `/share_create`, `/share_sync`, `/share_poll` (WS upgrade), `/share_data`, `/share_delete` — each resolving a DO stub via `SYNC_SERVER.get(idFromName(...))` and calling RPC methods (`share`, `publish`, `getData`, `clear`, `assertSecret`).

Notably it uses **classic (non-SQLite) DO storage** — the migration in `infra/app.ts` has `newSqliteClasses` commented out — which is the opposite choice from your `arche` app (where you went SQLite-backed). It also doesn't use Worker Loader / facets / dynamic plugins; it's a straightforward stateful coordinator + WS fan-out.

### Resource binding bridge

Two `Resource` shims adapt SST's linked resources depending on runtime:
- `resource.cloudflare.ts` — inside a Worker, reads bindings from the `cloudflare:workers` `env` proxy.
- `resource.node.ts` — in Node/dev, reimplements KV/R2 against the Cloudflare REST SDK (`new Cloudflare({ apiToken })`) so the same code works off-platform.

---

**TL;DR:** the binary launcher you pointed at touches no Cloudflare. In the CLI, Cloudflare appears as optional **Workers AI / AI Gateway model providers**. The real Cloudflare footprint is the **backend**: SST deploys Workers + R2 + KV + a `SyncServer` Durable Object (WebSocket-hibernation share sync, classic DO storage mirrored to R2). Want me to dig deeper into any one — e.g. the `SyncServer` share flow end-to-end, or how the providers get registered in `provider.ts`?

 Looking at how opencode integrates Cloudflare technology, the binary launcher itself doesn't use any Cloudflare tech, but the CLI supports Cloudflare as an LLM provider through options like `cloudflare-workers-ai` and `cloudflare-ai-gateway` that users can configure.

The webfetch tool handles Cloudflare bot detection by retrying requests with a proper user agent when it encounters challenges. The real infrastructure story is in the backend deployment—the entire opencode backend runs on Cloudflare via SST, with Workers handling the API, authentication, logging, and stats services, plus the SolidStart console and Astro docs site. Durable Objects power the real-time session sharing feature with WebSocket hibernation and storage, while R2 buckets store shared session data and KV handles auth tokens.

Great question — and there's a crucial distinction you need first, because opencode has **two completely separate "servers"** that are easy to conflate:

| | `opencode serve` (the docs page) | `SyncServer` Durable Object |
|---|---|---|
| What | The **agent runtime** as a headless HTTP server | A cloud **relay** for the "share session" feature |
| Where | **Local** — your laptop (`127.0.0.1:4096`) | **Cloudflare** edge |
| Runs the agent loop? | **Yes** — sessions, tools, model calls, LSP | **No** — pure storage + pub/sub |
| Cloudflare? | No, it's the native binary `bin/opencode` launches | Yes (Workers + DO + R2) |
| Optional? | It *is* opencode | Only used when you click "Share" |

The docs page you linked is entirely about the **first** one. The DO you've been looking at is the **second**. Let me cover both, then draw the lessons for your own build.

---

## 1. Purpose of the `SyncServer` Durable Object

It's a **per-shared-session cloud relay** for the optional Share feature (the public `opencode.ai/s/<id>` links). It runs **zero agent logic**. Its only job:

1. **Hold the latest session snapshot** for one shared session in DO storage (keyed via `idFromName(shortName)` — one DO instance per share).
2. **Mirror each write to R2** (`share/${key}.json`) so the snapshot is durably/statically readable.
3. **Fan out live updates over WebSocket** to every browser currently viewing that share URL.

This is the textbook DO use case: a **single consistent owner of one logical entity's state, that also holds open WebSocket connections**. Each shared session = one DO = one coordination point. (Note it uses *classic* DO storage, not SQLite — the opposite of your `arche` choice.)

## 2. How `opencode serve` works (the actual server-side architecture)

```
opencode serve [--port 4096] [--hostname 127.0.0.1] [--cors ...]
```

- Running `opencode` (TUI) **also** starts this server; the TUI is just a **client** talking to it over HTTP. So are the IDE plugins, the web UI, and the generated SDK. **One server, many clients.**

1. `revert.cleanup` (undo any pending reverted state)
2. `createUserMessage(input)` — the heavy lifting: resolves each input part (file → runs the Read tool inline; `agent` → task hint; MCP `resource` → fetches blob), runs the `chat.message` plugin hook, normalizes images, then **writes the user message + parts to SQLite** (`sessions.updateMessage` / `updatePart`).
